item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Echoing the others on wishing there was a more punchy ending, but flipping creationism on its head to make it a scientific anomaly is a nice idea. The reversal of stereotypical “religious man loses God in the face of science” is amusing to me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gah. Said it perfectly. I'm still waiting for the big finish. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just don't like this one… The concept of such a major event in such a long time period just not existing does not interest me at all, and the execution is just not good enough. Sorry, but of all 1300 SCPs I've read, this is my least favorite.
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All of this. For any dating system to work, processes must have continued: I think the idea is that nothing 'new' was created or happened, but that's not what the article features.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me a bit of Heribert Illig, who claims we're missing about 300 years of history because of a couple of misconceptions and miscalculations on his part. It's an interesting read, enough to upvote, but as a few others have said it's not as emotionally investing as a lot of others.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm, for all we know, all of history did not actually exist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for explaining this so well. This is exactly my point; some things have to keep going for there to be a noticeable gap, otherwise we'd have no way of realizing anything skipped.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was hooked and invested while reading throughout the containment procedures and description. I like the idea of such anomaly that is just a mere gap. However, the emails didn't do much and I've almost wanted to skip. I felt the emails are not suitable for this type of anomaly. The last addendum is pretty predictable too.

no vote, sorry. I think this got major potential, though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, the thing is, this doesn't imply that time didn't exist… Just that certain things weren't happening during that time.

For us to not see the stars in that distance range, but see the ones before and after, they must have not been producing light… But, light must have kept moving. Otherwise, we'd still see those stars' light, since it was frozen, and then started moving again.

And for the fossils not to be dated to that period, no fossils could have been created at the time… But, geological processes must have kept going. Otherwise the rock "older than that time period" would just look younger and there'd be no apparent problem.

Basically, for us to notice missing time, there must be something to compare to that's… Outside time, I guess. And you have provided things to compare to, but they're within time.

So, unless you come up with some different way for the Foundation to notice this, your current setup describes a time period during which some thinhs, but not all things, stopped happening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to upvote, but it's a really close call for me. The idea is brilliant, but the execution is terribly flawed. There's no punchline, no buildup, no real direction — and the skip seems empty, both of further ideas and of emotion.

I would beg you to rewrite and republish this later; if done well, it could be a stellar addition.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same here. You almost stick that landing but you didn't make it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanted to upvote so hard by the second paragraph of the description. Nthing that this is an interesting concept that falls short of providing a satisfying experience. The final revelation that there was a repeat event didn't do much to drive the story forward or provide any kind of fridge logic or insight that would have really gotten me invested. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a "gap" in time, so things happened before, and things happened after, but not during. There are fossils and rocks from the Permian, and fossils and rocks from the Triassic. There aren't any that date from the intervening period. As for the stars - when you look at a galaxy 250 million light years away, you're seeing light that was emitted 250 million light years ago. Since there was "nothing" there 252 million years ago, you can't see anything, but go further and the galaxies are visible because the event hasn't happened for them yet.

In real life, we believe that there was a rise in temperature and increase in UV radiation during that period, based largely on fossil records, and that's the same starting process here. Study of prehistory can consist of a lot of educated guesses and inferences, trying to paint a picture based on very scarce evidence. If anything, the Foundation scientists have evidence that is a lot more concrete and substantial than real world geologists and paleontologists do, and STILL need to add magical SCP confirmation because, well, reality is less plausible than fiction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As much as I like the idea of an entire time period not existing, the article only covers that, a few implications, and that this has happened again. It feels like a great base for something, though what's built off it doesn't do much to fuel the curiosity about what happened further or go any further direction with the idea.

Also, for more specific crit, I feel like the emails could be compressed into a single researcher note. Outside of the implications on the rest of history, there's nothing of importance in them.

Novoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a very cool idea but I confess to not quite getting it. If time didn't exist during that period, how could we tell? What would be different, assuming time resumed exactly where it left off?

The "stars distant from Earth" doesn't make much sense either, unless I'm missing something; what happened to the light during this time? Was it just deleted? Why can we see *further* back than that time?

An interesting idea, but either I'm missing something or the execution needs work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I REALLY like the idea behind it. However, there's no punchline or emotion that you end up with when you're finished. What's this whole anomaly's shtick in the first place. If the point is that the cause is unknowable, then 3098 is better. Overall, no vote. Add some more and you'll probably have an upvote from me.

item.title: Sources
item.content:encoded:
Image: Adopted from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Extinction_intensity.svg. Shared under CC 3.0 Share Alike.

Please note that part of the text describing mainstream science's understanding of the PTr extinction event was taken from Wikipedia. It can be changed if necessary.
